conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. 

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions about your circumstances. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have one child. My son Alex is 8 years old, and I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine if you're eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me ask you a few questions about your circumstances:

First, are you responsible for Alex? This means do you live with him and are you responsible for paying for things like his food, clothing, and other needs?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't live with me. He lives elsewhere, but I do help out financially when I can - I contribute towards his clothes and things like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. That's an important detail.

Let me ask: Who does Alex live with? Is it his other parent, another family member, or someone else?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He's actually in local authority care - he's been in a children's home for a while now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for sharing that information. I understand that must be a difficult situation.

To help me assess your eligibility properly, I need to ask: Does the local authority contribute towards the cost of looking after Alex while he's in care? And do you know if anyone else is currently claiming Child Benefit for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: The local authority is looking after him while he's there, yes. And no, I don't believe anyone else is claiming Child Benefit for him at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have a few more questions to help determine your eligibility:

How long has Alex been in the children's home? Has it been more than 8 weeks, or less than 8 weeks?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: It's been a couple of months now - so yes, more than 8 weeks.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for providing that information. Let me now read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information about your eligibility.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. I now have the information I need to assess your eligibility. I have just one final question:

Does Alex spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or does he spend 2 nights in a row each week or 7 days or more in a row at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't. He stays at the children's home full-time.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the official government guidance and the circumstances you've described, I need to inform you of my assessment:

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to inform you that you are **not eligible** to claim Child Benefit for Alex under these circumstances.

Here's why: When a child goes into local authority care for more than 8 weeks, Child Benefit usually stops. There are exceptions to this rule - for instance, if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you, or spends 2 nights in a row each week or 7 days or more in a row at home. However, since Alex stays at the children's home full-time and the local authority is paying towards his accommodation and maintenance, these exceptions don't apply in your situation.

While it's commendable that you contribute financially towards Alex's needs when you can, this doesn't meet the threshold for Child Benefit eligibility when a child is in full-time local authority care.

If Alex's circumstances change in the future - for example, if he starts spending regular time at home with you - you may wish to contact HMRC to reassess your eligibility at that time.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and does not live with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
  - Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05); Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
circumstances: Child does not live with claimant and weekly upkeep (£10.00) is below the Child Benefit rate (£26.05)
Child in local authority care for 12 weeks (>8) and does not spend 24+ hours/week at home
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and has been in local authority care (a children's home) for more than 8 weeks. According to the rules, Child Benefit usually stops after 8 weeks when a child goes into care. There are exceptions to this rule - for example, if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home, or spends 2 nights in a row each week or 7 days or more in a row at home. However, Alex stays at the children's home full-time and does not meet these exceptions. While you do contribute financially towards Alex's clothes and other needs when you can, the local authority is paying towards his accommodation and maintenance. Under these circumstances, you are not eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child (Alex, aged 8) who has been in local authority care for more than 8 weeks and stays there full-time. Under Child Benefit rules, payments usually stop after 8 weeks when a child goes into care, unless specific exceptions apply (such as the child spending regular significant time at home). These exceptions do not apply in this case, so the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit.
